common_name: Triomphe de Farcy Bean (French Haricot Vert Heirloom)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun; Triomphe de Farcy is one of the most important French heirloom haricot vert varieties — a fine-podded bush bean with elegant, slender pods featuring distinctive red-violet streaks and speckles on a green background (the markings fade when cooked), harvested at pencil-thin stage for the finest haricot vert quality.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost, 4–6 inches apart in rows 18 inches apart; harvest when pods are 4–5 inches long and pencil-thin for the finest haricot vert quality; pick every 2–3 days to maintain production; the red-violet streaking on raw pods fades to a uniform green when cooked. Triomphe de Farcy is a French heirloom bush bean dating to the 1880s, when it was listed in Vilmorin's famous seed catalog as one of the finest haricots verts — the slender, pencil-thin snap beans of French cuisine that differ fundamentally from the round, plump beans favored in American cooking. The variety is named for its triumphal quality (triomphe = triumph in French) and the Farcy region of Brittany where a similar bean type was cultivated. The pods are distinctive: slender (3/8 inch diameter at most), long (5–7 inches), and marked with beautiful irregular red-violet streaks and flecks on a pale green background — the coloration is reminiscent of a speckled trout and makes raw pods strikingly beautiful in the kitchen; the markings fade completely to solid green when cooked. The flavor is characteristically French haricot vert — tender, fine-grained, buttery, and subtly sweet — quite different from the starchier, coarser texture of standard American snap beans harvested at the same age. Triomphe de Farcy is the bean for gardeners who want authentic French-style haricots verts; harvest young and often, steam or blanch briefly, and serve with good butter.

common_name: Tongues of Fire Bean (Lingua di Fuoco, Italian Shell Bean Heirloom)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Tongues of Fire (Lingua di Fuoco in Italian) is a traditional Italian borlotto bean variety famous for its spectacular appearance — the pods are cream-colored with dramatic scarlet-red flame streaks (the 'tongues of fire') and the shelled beans within are cream-white with vivid red speckles that fade to a uniform brown when cooked; harvested as a shell bean or allowed to dry completely for a dry borlotto bean.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost, 4–6 inches apart in rows 18–24 inches apart; Tongues of Fire is a bush bean that can also be harvested as a snap bean at 5–6 inches (though the pods are somewhat coarse) — harvest as a shell bean when pods are plump and fully developed (60–70 days) or allow to dry completely for a dry borlotto bean (75–85 days). Tongues of Fire (Lingua di Fuoco) is a traditional Italian borlotto bean with some of the most dramatically beautiful pods in the vegetable garden — the medium-length pods are a creamy or pale yellow background completely covered with irregular streaks, splashes, and flames of scarlet-red that give the variety its evocative Italian name. The raw pods are so visually striking that harvesting them from the plant is genuinely exciting; unfortunately (from a visual standpoint), both the red pod markings and the speckled cream-and-red seed coloring fade completely when the beans are cooked, resulting in uniform tan to brownish seeds. The flavor of shell-stage Tongues of Fire beans is excellent — rich, earthy, and starchy in the characteristic Italian borlotto style that makes them the preferred bean for ribollita soup, pasta e fagioli, and other classic bean dishes of central and northern Italian cooking. Borlotti are used across Italy as the fresh shell bean equivalent of cranberry beans in New England cooking — they are always shelled before cooking, never eaten as whole pods. Tongues of Fire is reliable and productive in summer gardens throughout the US.

common_name: Red Noodle Yardlong Bean (Burgundy Asparagus Bean, Red Long Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost (yardlong beans are more heat-loving than Phaseolus beans and benefit from warmer soil, 70°F+); provide a trellis 6–8 feet tall; Red Noodle is the most visually dramatic yardlong bean variety, producing 18–24 inch pods of an extraordinary deep burgundy-crimson color that fade to purple-gray when cooked — the intensely colored pods are the most striking visual feature of any long bean grown in American gardens.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after the soil reaches 70°F or more (1–3 weeks after last frost) at the base of a sturdy trellis 6–8 feet tall; Red Noodle bean is extremely heat-tolerant and actually performs best in the hottest summer conditions — it continues producing during summer heat that stops most beans from setting pods. Red Noodle is a cultivar of the yardlong bean (also called asparagus bean, long bean, or snake bean), which is the same species as the cowpea (Vigna unguiculata) but selected for long pod production — the typical yardlong bean produces green pods 18–24 inches long; Red Noodle produces pods of the same length but in a spectacular deep burgundy to crimson-red color that is unique among commonly grown beans and makes the loaded trellis a visual centerpiece of the summer garden. The flavor is similar to standard green yardlong beans — somewhat starchier and with more umami than fresh Phaseolus beans — and is excellent stir-fried in Chinese cooking (the primary culinary tradition that uses yardlong beans), in pad thai, in Southeast Asian salads and pickles, or simply sautéed with garlic and soy. The dark color fades significantly when cooked, but remains visually interesting as a dark grayish-purple. Yardlong beans including Red Noodle are far more heat-tolerant than any Phaseolus bean variety and are ideal for Gulf Coast gardeners in zones 8b–10 who want continuous summer bean production through July and August when other beans fail.

common_name: Rat Tail Radish (Edible-Pod Radish, Podding Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in spring or summer; Rat Tail Radish is an unusual radish grown NOT for its root (which is tough and pithy) but for its edible seed pods — long (4–8 inch), slender, purple to green, crunchy pods that taste like radish with a snappy, peppery bite — and it tolerates the heat that causes standard radishes to bolt, making it productive throughout summer when root radishes fail.

directions: Sow directly 1/2 inch deep after last frost; allow plants to grow and bolt (flower); harvest pods when 3–6 inches long and still crisp and tender — before the seeds inside develop fully; harvest daily once production begins as pods become fibrous when overripe. Rat Tail Radish (Raphanus sativus var. caudatus) is a Southeast Asian and South Asian radish cultivated specifically for the edible seed pods it produces after bolting, rather than for the root — a striking reversal of the usual reason for growing radishes. When most radishes bolt, the gardener curses the heat and pulls the plant; with Rat Tail Radish, bolting is the point. The plants grow to 24–36 inches tall, produce small flowers (white or pale violet), and then develop long, slender, arching or pendant seed pods typically 4–8 inches long with a characteristic curved tip that gives the variety its common name. The pods are harvested young and tender (before full seed development) and have a crunchy, snappy texture and peppery, radish-flavored taste that is excellent raw in salads, pickled, stir-fried in Asian cooking, or used in Indian chutneys and curries where the peppery pod flavor is prized. The purple-podded forms (the most commonly grown) are visually dramatic and the pods are mildly ornamental on the plant. A practical advantage: Rat Tail Radish tolerates summer heat that destroys root radish production, providing fresh radish flavor throughout summer months.

common_name: München Bier Radish (German Beer Garden White Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow in spring 2–6 weeks before last frost, or in late summer for fall harvest; München Bier (Munich Beer) is a large, round, white-skinned German radish cultivated specifically for the traditional Bavarian custom of serving thick radish slices with beer — large (tennis ball to baseball-sized) round roots with pure white skin and flesh, mild-to-moderate radish bite, and a dense, juicy texture ideal for the accordion-sliced 'Radi' presentation traditional in Bavarian beer gardens.

directions: Sow 1/2 inch deep in cool weather; thin to 4–6 inches apart (important for large root development); keep consistently moist to prevent cracking; harvest when roots reach 2–3 inches in diameter for best flavor — overripe roots become pithy and hot. München Bier is a traditional Bavarian radish variety with an intimate connection to German beer garden culture: in Munich's famous outdoor beer gardens and throughout Bavaria, a 'Radi' (the Bavarian German word for radish) served alongside a Masskrug (liter) of beer is as traditional and culturally fixed as pretzels and sausages. The Bavarian Radi is always München Bier or a similar large, white, round variety — never the small red radishes of American experience — and it is served in a specific traditional way: the entire large root is scored with a spiral cut from top to bottom using a special tool (a Radihobel), accordioned apart to fan out into a long, beautiful spiral, rubbed with salt to draw out the juices, and eaten slice by slice as an accompaniment to beer. The flavor is mildly peppery and refreshing, with good crunch and juiciness, and the large size allows the elaborate spiral cutting presentation impossible with small radishes. München Bier is one of the few radish varieties specifically sized and textured for this presentation; it is also excellent sliced thick for salads, grated for German-style radish salads with vinegar and oil dressing, or pickled.
sources: German Federal Office for Plant Varieties (Bundessortenamt)
